⌂ Hub MATHS-III · LEARN & PRACTICE
U1U2U3U4U5
BS 901 MT · Semester 3 · the BME-specific paper

Mathematics – III
learn it, then practise it

The mathematics behind biomedical signals: complex numbers as a calculation tool, Fourier series to break a signal into its frequencies, and partial differential equations that model heat, waves and diffusion in the body.

How to use this page. This paper leans on Maths I & II — partial derivatives, integration, and series all come back. If a step feels shaky, revisit that lesson first, then return here. Work every example with a pen.

Unit I

Functions of a Complex Variable

Functions that eat and return complex numbers. The magic word is analytic — and one pair of equations decides everything.

1.1 Complex numbers recap1.2 Analytic & C–R equations1.3 Harmonic conjugate1.4 Cauchy's theorems1.5 Practice
Lesson 1.1

A quick recap of complex numbers

In plain words

A complex number is \(z=x+iy\), where \(i=\sqrt{-1}\). The \(x\) is the "real part", the \(y\) is the "imaginary part". A complex function \(f(z)\) also splits into two real pieces: \(f(z)=u(x,y)+i\,v(x,y)\).

So every complex function is really two real functions, \(u\) and \(v\), travelling together. Almost everything in this unit is about the relationship between them.

Lesson 1.2

Analytic functions and the Cauchy–Riemann equations

The idea

A function is analytic if it's smoothly differentiable throughout a region. Not every complex function is — and there's a clean test to check: the Cauchy–Riemann (C–R) equations. If \(u\) and \(v\) satisfy them, the function is analytic.

Cauchy–Riemann equations \[u_x=v_y\qquad\text{and}\qquad u_y=-v_x\]

Watch the minus sign in the second equation — it's the classic slip.

Method — test analyticity

  1. Split \(f(z)\) into \(u(x,y)\) and \(v(x,y)\).
  2. Compute the four partials \(u_x,\ u_y,\ v_x,\ v_y\).
  3. Check whether \(u_x=v_y\) and \(u_y=-v_x\). If both hold, it's analytic.

Worked example 1.2a standard

Show that \(f(z)=z^{2}\) is analytic.

Split into u and v \(z^2=(x+iy)^2=(x^2-y^2)+i(2xy)\), so \(u=x^2-y^2,\ v=2xy\).

Partials \(u_x=2x,\ u_y=-2y,\ v_x=2y,\ v_y=2x\).

Check C–R \(u_x=2x=v_y\) ✓, and \(u_y=-2y=-v_x\) ✓.

Conclude Both hold, so \(f\) is analytic everywhere, with \(f'(z)=u_x+iv_x=2x+i2y=2z\).

Lesson 1.3

Harmonic functions and finding the conjugate

The idea

For an analytic function, both \(u\) and \(v\) automatically satisfy Laplace's equation \(u_{xx}+u_{yy}=0\) — such functions are called harmonic. A favourite exam task: you're given \(u\), and asked to find its partner \(v\) (the "harmonic conjugate").

Worked example 1.3a find the conjugate

Given \(u=x^{2}-y^{2}\), find the harmonic conjugate \(v\).

Use the first C–R equation \(v_y=u_x=2x\). Integrate in \(y\): \(v=2xy+g(x)\).

Use the second C–R equation \(v_x=2y+g'(x)\), and this must equal \(-u_y=-(-2y)=2y\).

Solve for g \(2y+g'(x)=2y\Rightarrow g'(x)=0\Rightarrow g=\text{constant}\).

Conclusion \(v=2xy\) (so \(f=u+iv=z^2\), matching Lesson 1.2).

Lesson 1.4

Cauchy's integral theorem and formula

The idea

Analytic functions are so well-behaved that integrating one around any closed loop gives exactly zero. And if there's a single "bad point" inside the loop, a neat formula reads off the function's value there.

The two results

Cauchy's integral theorem: if \(f\) is analytic inside and on a closed curve \(C\), then \(\displaystyle\oint_C f(z)\,dz=0\).

Cauchy's integral formula: \(\displaystyle\oint_C\frac{f(z)}{z-a}\,dz=2\pi i\,f(a)\), when \(a\) is inside \(C\).

Worked example 1.4a using the formula

Evaluate \(\displaystyle\oint_{|z|=2}\frac{z}{z-1}\,dz\).

Match the formula Here \(f(z)=z\) and \(a=1\). Is \(a=1\) inside \(|z|=2\)? Yes.

Apply \(\oint=2\pi i\,f(1)=2\pi i\cdot 1=2\pi i\).

Unit I don't-lose-marks list
  • Second C–R equation has a minus: \(u_y=-v_x\).
  • Cauchy's formula only applies when the point \(a\) is inside the curve.
  • If the function is analytic everywhere inside, the integral is simply \(0\).

Practice — Unit I

For analyticity, always write out all four partials before checking.

Q1

Verify the C–R equations for \(f(z)=z^3\) at least in form — is it analytic? (Hint: any polynomial in \(z\) is.)

SOLUTION Every polynomial in \(z\) is analytic everywhere; \(f'(z)=3z^2\). (You can confirm via C–R by expanding \(z^3\).)

Analytic everywhere.

Q2

Given \(u=x\), find its harmonic conjugate \(v\).

SOLUTION \(v_y=u_x=1\Rightarrow v=y+g(x)\). \(v_x=g'(x)=-u_y=0\Rightarrow g=\)const.

\(v=y\) (so \(f=x+iy=z\)).

Q3

Evaluate \(\displaystyle\oint_{|z|=1}\frac{e^{z}}{z}\,dz\).

SOLUTION \(f(z)=e^z,\ a=0\) (inside). By Cauchy's formula \(=2\pi i\,e^0=2\pi i\).

\(2\pi i\).

Q4

Evaluate \(\displaystyle\oint_{|z|=1} z^{2}\,dz\).

SOLUTION \(z^2\) is analytic everywhere inside the circle, so by Cauchy's theorem the integral is zero.

\(0\).

Unit II

Residue Calculus

The clever trick that turns hard integrals into simple bookkeeping over the "bad points" of a function.

2.1 Taylor & Laurent series2.2 Poles & singularities2.3 Residues2.4 Residue theorem2.5 Practice
Lesson 2.1

Taylor and Laurent series

The idea

A Taylor series writes a function as a sum of positive powers of \((z-a)\) — good where the function is well-behaved. A Laurent series also allows negative powers like \(\tfrac{1}{z-a}\) — needed near a "bad point". Those negative-power terms are exactly what we'll harvest as residues.

Lesson 2.2

Singularities and poles

In plain words

A singularity is a point where a function blows up (denominator becomes zero). The commonest kind is a pole. If \(\tfrac{1}{z-a}\) appears, \(z=a\) is a "simple pole" (order 1); \(\tfrac{1}{(z-a)^2}\) is a pole of order 2, and so on.

Worked example 2.2a locate poles

Find the poles of \(f(z)=\dfrac{z}{(z-1)(z-3)}\).

Set denominator to zero \((z-1)(z-3)=0\Rightarrow z=1\) and \(z=3\).

Order Each factor appears once, so both are simple poles.

Lesson 2.3

Residues

The idea

The residue at a pole is a single number that captures the "leftover" of the function there. For a simple pole it's easy to compute with a limit.

Residue at a simple pole \(z=a\) \[\operatorname{Res}_{z=a}f=\lim_{z\to a}(z-a)\,f(z)\]

Worked example 2.3a standard

Find the residue of \(f(z)=\dfrac{z}{(z-1)(z-3)}\) at \(z=1\).

Multiply out the pole \((z-1)f=\dfrac{z}{z-3}\).

Take the limit \(z\to1\) \(\dfrac{1}{1-3}=-\dfrac12\).

Result \(\operatorname{Res}_{z=1}f=-\tfrac12\).

Lesson 2.4

The residue theorem

The idea

To integrate around a closed loop, you don't integrate at all — you just add up the residues of the poles trapped inside, and multiply by \(2\pi i\). That's the residue theorem, and it's what makes this unit powerful.

Residue theorem \[\oint_C f(z)\,dz=2\pi i\sum(\text{residues of poles inside }C)\]

Worked example 2.4a full problem

Evaluate \(\displaystyle\oint_{|z|=2}\frac{z}{(z-1)(z-3)}\,dz\).

Which poles are inside? \(z=1\) is inside \(|z|=2\); \(z=3\) is outside. Only \(z=1\) counts.

Residue at \(z=1\) From Lesson 2.3, it's \(-\tfrac12\).

Apply the theorem \(\oint=2\pi i\left(-\tfrac12\right)=-\pi i\).

Unit II don't-lose-marks list
  • Only include poles inside the contour — always check against the circle's radius.
  • For a pole of order \(m>1\), the simple-pole limit formula does not apply — use the derivative version.

Practice — Unit II

First find the poles, then decide which are inside, then sum their residues.

Q1

Find the residue of \(\dfrac{1}{z-2}\) at \(z=2\).

SOLUTION \(\lim_{z\to2}(z-2)\cdot\dfrac{1}{z-2}=1\).

\(1\).

Q2

Find the residue of \(\dfrac{z}{z^2+1}\) at \(z=i\). (Note \(z^2+1=(z-i)(z+i)\).)

SOLUTION \((z-i)f=\dfrac{z}{z+i}\); at \(z=i\): \(\dfrac{i}{2i}=\tfrac12\).

\(\tfrac12\).

Q3

Evaluate \(\displaystyle\oint_{|z|=1}\frac{1}{z}\,dz\).

SOLUTION Pole at \(z=0\) (inside), residue \(1\). Integral \(=2\pi i(1)\).

\(2\pi i\).

Q4

Evaluate \(\displaystyle\oint_{|z|=3}\frac{z}{(z-1)(z-3)}\,dz\). (Careful with the radius!)

SOLUTION \(|z|=3\) passes through \(z=3\) — the pole sits on the contour, so the residue theorem does not apply directly (the integral is improper). Only \(z=1\) is strictly inside; examiners usually intend \(|z|=2\) here. Flag the boundary issue.

Ill-defined as written — pole on the contour.

Unit III

Fourier Series

Breaking a repeating signal into a sum of simple sine and cosine waves — the exact idea behind analysing an ECG or EEG.

3.1 The big idea3.2 The coefficient formulas3.3 Even & odd shortcuts3.4 Half-range series3.5 Practice
Lesson 3.1

The big idea

In plain words

Any repeating (periodic) function — however jagged — can be built by adding together sine and cosine waves of different frequencies. A Fourier series tells you exactly how much of each wave to add. The "how much" numbers are called the Fourier coefficients.

Fourier series on \((-\pi,\pi)\) \[f(x)=\frac{a_0}{2}+\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\big(a_n\cos nx+b_n\sin nx\big)\]
Lesson 3.2

The coefficient formulas

The three formulas \[a_0=\frac{1}{\pi}\int_{-\pi}^{\pi}f(x)\,dx,\quad a_n=\frac{1}{\pi}\int_{-\pi}^{\pi}f(x)\cos nx\,dx,\quad b_n=\frac{1}{\pi}\int_{-\pi}^{\pi}f(x)\sin nx\,dx\]

That's the whole method: plug the given \(f(x)\) into these three integrals, and the series is built. The integration usually needs integration by parts, which is where care pays off.

Lesson 3.3

Even and odd shortcuts

The idea — huge time-saver

If \(f\) is even (symmetric, like \(x^2\)), all the sine coefficients \(b_n\) vanish — only cosines survive. If \(f\) is odd (like \(x\)), all the cosine coefficients \(a_n\) (and \(a_0\)) vanish — only sines survive. Always check symmetry first; it can halve the work.

Worked example 3.3a odd function

Find the Fourier series of \(f(x)=x\) on \((-\pi,\pi)\).

Symmetry \(x\) is odd, so \(a_0=0\) and all \(a_n=0\). Only \(b_n\) needed.

Compute \(b_n\) \(b_n=\dfrac{2}{\pi}\displaystyle\int_0^{\pi}x\sin nx\,dx\). By parts: \(=\dfrac{2}{\pi}\left[-\dfrac{x\cos nx}{n}+\dfrac{\sin nx}{n^2}\right]_0^{\pi}\).

Evaluate \(\sin n\pi=0,\ \cos n\pi=(-1)^n\), so \(b_n=\dfrac{2}{\pi}\cdot\left(-\dfrac{\pi(-1)^n}{n}\right)=\dfrac{2(-1)^{n+1}}{n}\).

Series \(x=2\displaystyle\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\dfrac{(-1)^{n+1}}{n}\sin nx\).

Lesson 3.4

Half-range series

The idea

Sometimes a function is only defined on \((0,\pi)\) and you're asked for a sine-only or cosine-only expansion. The trick: mentally extend the function to \((-\pi,\pi)\) as odd (for a sine series) or even (for a cosine series), then use the usual formulas.

Unit III don't-lose-marks list
  • Test even/odd first — it can kill a whole family of coefficients.
  • Integration by parts on \(x\sin nx\) or \(x\cos nx\) is where sign errors creep in.
  • For half-range, choose odd extension → sine series; even extension → cosine series.

Practice — Unit III

Decide even/odd before touching an integral.

Q1

For \(f(x)=x^2\) on \((-\pi,\pi)\), which coefficients vanish?

SOLUTION \(x^2\) is even, so all sine coefficients vanish.

All \(b_n=0\); only \(a_0,a_n\) remain.

Q2

For \(f(x)=x^3\) on \((-\pi,\pi)\), which coefficients vanish?

SOLUTION \(x^3\) is odd, so \(a_0=0\) and all \(a_n=0\).

Only \(b_n\) survive.

Q3

Compute \(a_0\) for \(f(x)=x^2\) on \((-\pi,\pi)\).

SOLUTION \(a_0=\dfrac1\pi\int_{-\pi}^{\pi}x^2\,dx=\dfrac1\pi\cdot\dfrac{2\pi^3}{3}=\dfrac{2\pi^2}{3}\).

\(a_0=\dfrac{2\pi^2}{3}\).

Unit IV

Partial Differential Equations

Equations with an unknown function of several variables and its partial derivatives. First form them, then solve the first-order ones.

4.1 What a PDE is4.2 Forming a PDE4.3 Lagrange's method4.4 Charpit's method4.5 Practice
Lesson 4.1

What a PDE is (and the p, q shorthand)

In plain words

A partial differential equation involves an unknown function \(z(x,y)\) and its partial derivatives. We use shorthand: \(p=\dfrac{\partial z}{\partial x}\) and \(q=\dfrac{\partial z}{\partial y}\). So an equation might read \(p+q=z\).

Lesson 4.2

Forming a PDE

The idea

PDEs are often built by eliminating arbitrary constants (or functions) from a given relation, using differentiation. You need as many differentiations as there are constants to remove.

Worked example 4.2a form a PDE

Form a PDE by eliminating \(a,b\) from \(z=ax+by+ab\).

Differentiate \(p=\dfrac{\partial z}{\partial x}=a\), and \(q=\dfrac{\partial z}{\partial y}=b\).

Substitute back Since \(a=p,\ b=q\), the relation \(z=ax+by+ab\) becomes:

PDE \(z=px+qy+pq\).

Lesson 4.3

Lagrange's linear equation

The idea

A first-order linear PDE has the form \(Pp+Qq=R\). Lagrange's method solves it by turning it into a set of ordinary "auxiliary" fractions, solving two of them, and combining.

Lagrange's method

For \(Pp+Qq=R\), solve the auxiliary equations \(\dfrac{dx}{P}=\dfrac{dy}{Q}=\dfrac{dz}{R}\). Get two independent solutions \(u=c_1,\ v=c_2\); the general solution is \(\phi(u,v)=0\).

Worked example 4.3a standard

Solve \(y\,p-x\,q=0\).

Auxiliary equations \(\dfrac{dx}{y}=\dfrac{dy}{-x}=\dfrac{dz}{0}\).

From the last ratio \(dz=0\Rightarrow z=c_1\).

From the first pair \(x\,dx+y\,dy=0\Rightarrow x^2+y^2=c_2\).

General solution \(z=F(x^2+y^2)\) — the surface depends only on distance from the axis.

Lesson 4.4

Charpit's method (for non-linear PDEs)

The idea

When the first-order PDE is non-linear in \(p\) and \(q\), Lagrange's method won't work. Charpit's method is the general procedure: it builds an auxiliary system to find an extra relation between \(p\) and \(q\), which you then integrate.

Recognising that a problem needs Charpit (non-linear) rather than Lagrange (linear) is the first, examinable decision.

Unit IV don't-lose-marks list
  • Match \(P,Q,R\) carefully into \(\tfrac{dx}{P}=\tfrac{dy}{Q}=\tfrac{dz}{R}\).
  • When forming a PDE, count the arbitrary constants — that's how many derivatives you need.
  • Linear in \(p,q\) → Lagrange; non-linear → Charpit.

Practice — Unit IV

For Lagrange, always write the three auxiliary fractions first.

Q1

Form a PDE by eliminating \(a,b\) from \(z=ax+by\).

SOLUTION \(p=a,\ q=b\), so \(z=px+qy\).

\(z=px+qy\).

Q2

Solve \(p+q=1\) using \(z=px+qy+\ldots\) reasoning (a complete integral).

SOLUTION Take \(p=a\), then \(q=1-a\). A complete integral is \(z=ax+(1-a)y+c\).

\(z=ax+(1-a)y+c\).

Q3

Solve \(xp+yq=z\) by Lagrange's method.

SOLUTION \(\dfrac{dx}{x}=\dfrac{dy}{y}=\dfrac{dz}{z}\). First pair: \(y/x=c_1\). Second: \(z/x=c_2\).

\(z=x\,F(y/x)\).

Unit V

Fourier Series Applied to PDEs

The payoff: solving the heat, wave and Laplace equations by splitting variables and rebuilding with a Fourier series.

5.1 The three equations5.2 Separation of variables5.3 Worked heat equation5.4 Practice
Lesson 5.1

The three classic equations

In plain words

Three PDEs describe an enormous amount of physics — and of biomedical engineering.

The three equations

Heat (diffusion, e.g. thermal therapy): \(\dfrac{\partial u}{\partial t}=c^2\dfrac{\partial^2 u}{\partial x^2}\).

Wave (vibration, ultrasound): \(\dfrac{\partial^2 u}{\partial t^2}=c^2\dfrac{\partial^2 u}{\partial x^2}\).

Laplace (steady state): \(\dfrac{\partial^2 u}{\partial x^2}+\dfrac{\partial^2 u}{\partial y^2}=0\).

Lesson 5.2

The separation-of-variables method

The idea

Guess that the solution splits into a product: \(u(x,t)=X(x)\,T(t)\), one factor per variable. Substituting turns the PDE into two ordinary differential equations — which you already know how to solve from Maths II. Then a Fourier series stitches the pieces together to fit the boundary conditions.

Method — separation of variables

  1. Assume \(u=X(x)T(t)\) and substitute into the PDE.
  2. Rearrange so each side depends on one variable only; set both equal to a constant \(-\lambda\).
  3. Solve the two resulting ODEs, applying the boundary conditions to fix the constants.
  4. Add up (superpose) the solutions as a Fourier series to match the initial condition.
Lesson 5.3

Worked walk-through: the heat equation

Worked example 5.3a the method in action

Outline the solution of \(u_t=c^2u_{xx}\) on a rod \(0\le x\le L\) with ends held at zero, \(u(0,t)=u(L,t)=0\).

Separate Put \(u=X(x)T(t)\). Substituting and dividing: \(\dfrac{T'}{c^2T}=\dfrac{X''}{X}=-\lambda\).

Space equation \(X''+\lambda X=0\) with \(X(0)=X(L)=0\) gives \(X_n=\sin\dfrac{n\pi x}{L}\) and \(\lambda_n=\left(\dfrac{n\pi}{L}\right)^2\).

Time equation \(T'=-c^2\lambda_n T\Rightarrow T_n=e^{-c^2\lambda_n t}\).

Superpose \(u(x,t)=\displaystyle\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}b_n\sin\dfrac{n\pi x}{L}\,e^{-c^2\lambda_n t}\), with \(b_n\) fixed by expanding the initial temperature as a Fourier sine series.

Unit V don't-lose-marks list
  • The separation constant is written \(-\lambda\); the wrong sign gives exponentials where you need sines.
  • Apply boundary conditions first (they fix the shapes), the initial condition last (it fixes the coefficients).

Practice — Unit V

These are mostly "set up and outline" questions — showing the method structure earns most of the marks.

Q1

Name the type of each: (a) \(u_t=c^2u_{xx}\); (b) \(u_{tt}=c^2u_{xx}\); (c) \(u_{xx}+u_{yy}=0\).

SOLUTION (a) heat/diffusion; (b) wave; (c) Laplace (steady state).

Q2

In separation of variables for \(u_t=c^2u_{xx}\), what two ODEs do you get from \(u=X(x)T(t)\)?

SOLUTION \(X''+\lambda X=0\) and \(T'+c^2\lambda T=0\).

Q3

With ends at \(x=0,L\) held at zero, what are the allowed \(X_n(x)\)?

SOLUTION The zero-endpoint conditions force sine shapes.

\(X_n=\sin\dfrac{n\pi x}{L}\).

Your weekly routine
  • Read one unit's lessons, working every example by hand.
  • Practise that unit with solutions hidden; re-do only the ones you missed.
  • Build a one-page formula sheet per unit (C–R equations, residue formula, Fourier coefficients, Lagrange's auxiliary system).
  • Finish with matching questions from an Osmania University past paper.

About this page

A beginner-friendly, worked tutorial for Engineering Mathematics-III (BS 901 MT), BE Biomedical Engineering, University College of Engineering (Autonomous), Osmania University. Explanations, examples and practice are standard treatments aligned to the prescribed texts (Grewal; Jain & Iyengar; Kreyszig; Brown & Churchill). Equations render with MathJax; tap "Show solution" to check your practice answers.

Self-contained single file — upload index.html to tiiny.host to publish. Needs an internet connection to load web fonts and the MathJax renderer.  Compiled study reference, not an official university publication.  Prepared 04 July 2026.
